name: Log Analysis with journalctl
description: Search, filter, and analyze systemd journal logs to diagnose service failures, boot issues, and system errors.

steps.title: View logs for a specific service
steps.description: Show all logs from a specific systemd service.

steps.title: Follow logs in real time
steps.description: Stream new log entries as they are written.

steps.title: Filter logs by time range
steps.description: View logs from a specific time period.
steps.tip: Priority levels: emerg, alert, crit, err, warning, notice, info, debug. Use --priority=err to see only errors and above.

steps.title: View boot logs
steps.description: Show logs from the current or a previous boot.
steps.tip: -b 0 is the current boot, -b -1 is the previous boot. Use --list-boots to see available boot IDs.

steps.title: Check journal disk usage
steps.description: See how much disk space the journal logs are consuming.
steps.tip: Use 'sudo journalctl --vacuum-size=500M' to reduce journal size to 500MB.

fullScript:
#!/bin/bash
# Log Analysis with journalctl
set -euo pipefail

echo "=== Journal Disk Usage ==="
journalctl --disk-usage

echo ""
echo "=== Recent Errors (last hour) ==="
journalctl --since '1 hour ago' --priority=err --no-pager -n 20 2>/dev/null || echo "No errors"

echo ""
echo "=== Available Boots ==="
journalctl --list-boots | tail -5

echo ""
echo "=== Failed Services Logs ==="
for service in $(systemctl list-units --type=service --state=failed --no-legend | awk '{print $1}'); do
  echo "--- $service ---"
  journalctl -u "$service" --no-pager -n 10
  echo ""
done

echo ""
echo "=== Log Commands ==="
echo "Service:    journalctl -u <service> -f"
echo "Errors:     journalctl --priority=err --since '1h ago'"
echo "Boot:       journalctl -b -1"
echo "Kernel:     journalctl -k"
echo "Vacuum:     sudo journalctl --vacuum-size=500M"

faq.question: How do I make journal logs persistent across reboots?
faq.answer: Create the persistent storage directory: 'sudo mkdir -p /var/log/journal'. Then restart journald: 'sudo systemctl restart systemd-journald'. Logs will persist across reboots.

faq.question: Can I export journal logs to a file?
faq.answer: Yes. Use 'journalctl -u nginx --since today > nginx.log' for text, or 'journalctl -u nginx -o json > nginx.json' for structured JSON output.
